body.p:
The rule baseConceptsRule Base of XSLT can probably accommodate an infinite number of rules, if we disregard our hardware limitations. However, the programmer is responsible for the completeness, uniqueness and consistency of the rule base.
UWE test footnote (8/40, callout H): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
UWE supplement (fn-add) for the footnote index.
To ensure the uniqueness of rules, there are various mechanisms.

section.title: Order of Match Rules
section.p: Normally, exactly one rule should match a specific node in a specific scenario. If there is a conflict, at least with Saxon, the rule that was declared last in the stylesheet is used.

Knowing this circumstance is important precisely when you have to take over existing stylesheet code. Following the motto "Never change a running system", you should clean things up very carefully in this regard.

section.title: Evaluation Precedence
section.p: Match rulesConceptsMatch Rules are sorted according to their specificity, and the one that best applies to a node in a given scenario is used for evaluation. Generally, the rules are sorted according to the following criteria:ConceptsPriority

li.p: Imported template rules always have a lower priority than the rules of the importing stylesheet.
Templates with a higher priority attribute take precedence.
Templates without a priority are automatically assigned a default priority. The highest default priority is 0.5.
This default priority is calculated based on the conditions or wildcards attached to a match rule:
If multiple templates match, the most specific one is used for evaluation.
The most specific template is determined based on the priorities.
Node tests for other nodes (e.g. comment(), node(), etc.) have priority -0.5
In all other cases the priority is 0.5.
With a command line option in Saxon, you can specify that the transformation aborts as soon as there is a conflict in rule evaluationConceptsRule Evaluation .

body.section:
Import and Default Rule
As stated in point 1 of the section above, allConceptsImport Precedence rules in an imported stylesheet have a lower priority than those in the importing stylesheet. This circumstance can be exploited to include a default ruleConceptsDefault Rule , for example: <xsl:template match="*" mode="#all"/>
Since this rule is in an imported stylesheet, it has lower priority than all other rules and only applies when no other match rules are defined for a traversed node.
This is practical, for example, to identify unhandled elements - for this purpose, the above rule would not be empty but would, for instance, write a yellow-highlighted warning text directly into the output format. | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , .
An empty default rule, on the other hand, is useful when, for example, nodes in the XML tree should be automatically trimmed in an XML-to-XML migration...

section.title: Priorities
section.p: AllConceptsPriority match rules are assigned a priority. The stylesheet developer has the option to override this priority. The attribute [[code:@priority]] on the match rule is used for this purpose. A use case for priorities would be, for example, the following filter scenario

li.p: The input instance should be filtered in a preprocessing step.
Seminar elements that do not meet special conditions should be marked:
The seminar element has an "end date" field that has expired.
Multiple instructors are assigned to the seminar element, even though the seminar element is of type "Single".
No instructor is assigned to the seminar element.
There may certainly be seminar elements that meet all three conditions. However, to avoid overfilling the error log, the filters should be executed according to their priorities.

section.p: Translated into XSLT templates, this requirement could be implemented as follows:
section.pre:
<xsl:template match="Seminar[Ende-Datum/xs:date(.) le current-date()]" priority="30" mode="filter-seminare"> <xsl:element name="Filtered-Seminar" namespace="{namespace-uri()}"> <xsl:attribute name="reason">termed-seminar</xsl:attribute> <xsl:apply-templates select="node()|@*" mode="filter-seminare"/> </xsl:element> </xsl:template>
<xsl:template match="Seminar[Type eq 'SINGLE' and count(dozenten/dozent) gt 1]" priority="20" mode="filter-seminare"> <xsl:element name="filtered-Seminar" namespace="{namespace-uri()}"> <xsl:attribute name="reason">dozenten-count</xsl:attribute> <xsl:apply-templates select="node()|@*" mode="filter-seminare"/> </xsl:element> </xsl:template>
<xsl:template match="Seminar[not(dozenten/dozent)]" mode="filter-seminare"> <xsl:element name="filtered-Seminar" namespace="{namespace-uri()}"> <xsl:attribute name="reason">dozenten-missing</xsl:attribute> <xsl:apply-templates select="node()|@*" mode="filter-seminare"/> </xsl:element> </xsl:template>
section.p:
In addition to the use of the @priority attributeXSLT ConstructsPriority Attribute
Attributespriority and the @mode attribute described below, it is also interesting to note that the filtered seminar elements here are not deleted but renamed. This way, they can be further processed in a subsequent transformation step (keyword: Pre-Transformations ) without interfering with the regular processing.

body.section:
Mode Attributes On all templates you have the option to specify a self-declared mode. When the XSLT processor is then pushed in a certain direction, cf. Push vs. Pull Stylesheets, only those rules are used for evaluation that are in the same mode as the apply-templates call itself. For example, you might want to treat titles in chapters differently than titles in the table of contents, because in the TOC, for instance, no footnote markers should be displayed. Expressed in templates, such an instruction would look like this:

The TOC generation could then proceed as follows: | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . | | Rejected by , . Regarding the uniqueness of the rule base, you can thus form execution groups using the mode attributeXSLT ConstructsMode Attribute Attributesmode . As with specifying priorities, in this way you can set rules that are never executed because they were perhaps disconnected during a refactoring measure and then forgotten. The [[code:mode]] attribute should therefore also be used with caution and sparingly.
